summary: Gruber explains the technical setup behind his remotely-shot Talk Show episode from WWDC 2020 with Craig Federighi and Greg Joswiak. The key insight is deceptively simple: they conducted a Webex call so participants could see and hear each other, but none of that call's footage was used in the final product. Instead, they recorded audio locally with professional microphones and shot 4K video on iPhone 11 Pros positioned just behind the conference call devices. The post-production editing was handled by Sandwich, the video production company. Gruber emphasizes that the technique requires no expensive equipment — just good microphones, iPhones, and the discipline to record everything locally rather than relying on internet call quality.
key_points: The conference call (Webex) was used solely for live communication between participants — none of its audio or video appeared in the final show
All video was shot in 4K on iPhone 11 Pros, with the cameras positioned just behind and above the conference call device to create a natural 'looking at camera' angle
Audio was recorded locally with professional microphones for each participant, using the podcasting technique called 'double-ending'
The secret to a professional-looking remote interview is simply not using the internet call footage at all — record everything locally with decent equipment
Post-production by Sandwich was the 'cheat code,' but the raw iPhone footage was genuinely good on its own
money_quotes: The bad news is it's a lot of work.
Zoom is kicking their ass as the go-to app for remote meetings because Zoom makes easy what Webex makes confusing.
One advantage of using iPads is that you guarantee there will be no fan noise.
The trick to getting that 'looking right at the camera' angle is to position the iPhone camera just behind and above the device being used for the conference call.
Calling in Sandwich for post-production and editing was, let's face it, a cheat code, but the raw video footage from the iPhones was really good.
Recent iPhones are amazingly good video cameras.
Basically, the secret to shooting a remote interview that doesn't look like a recorded internet call is simply not to use the internet call video.
key_insight: The secret to a professional remote interview is the podcasting principle of 'double-ending' applied to both audio and video — record everything locally with decent equipment and never rely on the internet call footage.
